speaker: interviewer
text: Your grandmother-- when was she born, Halina?

speaker: interviewee
text: As far as I know, it was 1860, and she died in 1935-- and in Krakow, of course. And she was-- she lived with us and she was-- when she married, Fabian Barber, my grandfather, he was about 65, and she was 18. She was his youngest daughter-- he sent for a wife, I'm sorry. It was friend of his youngest daughter. And then-- but he was this one who was such a fighter for, well, Polish independence, and he also was a quite well-to-do gentleman. And he built that house for Grandma, and in this house was my mother born, and I was born, and my brother. As a matter of fact, the house was taken away from us, first by the Germans, then by the communists. Until now, I cannot even get our house back in Krakow. But Grandma was living with us, and we were living in-- you see, every tenant in this house, it was living from generation to generation. We were all friends. When I came to visit, visit to see what is going on about 15 years ago or so, there was no one of the old tenants there. No one. They even hardly allowed me to go, and we had the whole second floor. And to take a look at my place where I was born. But anyway, Grandma had five children, and the oldest was the uncle, engineer, Uncle Ignacy, who was also in Krakow and had two children, two boys, my cousins. And then he was much older than my mother, and he perished in ghetto Krakow during that October transport, probably, I don't know where, to Sobibor or to else, and together with his youngest son.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year?

speaker: interviewer
text: And when did your-- could you spell your grandmother's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Rosaelia, R-O-S-A-E-L-I-A Barber, B-A-R-B-E-R. And that uncle was Ignacy, I-G-N-A-C-Y.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your grandfather?

speaker: interviewee
text: Grandfather was Fabian. F-A-B-I-A-N. Fabian Barber.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your grandmother's maiden name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Was Herstein, H-E-R-S-T-E-I-N.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And your grandmother died when?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1935, in Krakow. And-- but then, she had-- my mother was the youngest of her children. The oldest was that uncle, and then there were three daughters, and was Hella, who lived and was married in Vienna, and Bronka, who was in Hungary and later on in Czechoslovakia, and Lola, who was in Slovakia. And all of them and their children and everything perished in Theresienstadt during the Second World War. Except two children of one of the-- Lola, who escaped to Palestine. And I lost contact with them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know about when they perished?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. They were in Theresienstadt, probably as long as Theresienstadt existed. And then they were probably taken all to-- unless they died, just died, they were sent to Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your-- I beg your pardon, your grandfather passed away?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, that grandfather passed away still in 19th century, at the end of the--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Let us move to the first signs that there was danger ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: The first sign that there was danger ahead, it was just a few days before September 1, 1939.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you when you first--

speaker: interviewee
text: In Krakow. First, we were on vacation, as usually, in summer. But then we came back home and then there was really that feeling of mobilization and soldiers and this and people moving back and forth. And then we had to do-- keep our windows dark and everything prepared against an air raid and so forth. But while they were also saying, don't worry, the Germans have towns made out of cardboard, there is nothing to worry about. We are strong, we will give them. [DRINKS] I remember when I woke up on the 1st of September, 1939, it was so dark because there was the black shades in the window. And I opened the window, and there was beautiful early morning in fall, beautiful. And it was so quiet and so nice and I felt so relieved. And then later on, I had that feeling that the whole world kept their breath-- their breath, suddenly. And suddenly, I heard some strange noise. And now more and more and more-- and they were the bombers. And there was when the first bombs fell on Krakow.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old were you, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Now-- well, and nobody knew exactly what was going on. Well, this was '39, so I was about 13, almost 14. And then, there was this enormous with people afterwards-- nobody knew what was going on, whether there is a real war. But then we heard from the radio that we are being invaded, and-- and what is going on? And that men should go to the east. I looked my father went to, to-- well, for the mobilization place. But then they said, every man should go, and we were being evacuated. His bank was evacuated.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we were evacuated with-- with them. And this is the reason our nanny stayed in home, and we left with Father. Of course, nobody could find that place where they were supposed to get uniforms, and this and that.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it was incredible to go with the masses of people and soldiers, and everybody was frightened. Nobody knew what is going on. And we were really leaving and going with the-- it was like, like, you know, surge of people. There was no way to go back-- everybody was afraid of the Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: So evacuated to where?

speaker: interviewee
text: To the east. Everywhere was, well, that Polish army was moving to the east and through Romania and Hungary, later onwards in France, to England. And-- and everybody was going, too. There only Warsaw-- stay, stop the Germans for about six weeks, and was-- there was the fighting and the battle. But here, there was nothing. They were-- all the time, the airplanes, the German airplanes, were flying overhead and they were bombing us and we were like sitting ducks. Not even sitting ducks-- we were just-- I remember in one ditch, waiting while everybody wanted just to get into the earth. And I look, and on the leaves of grass, there was a little ant walking with something. And the ant was walking this way and that way and the other way, and finally went up the grass all around. And I said, how strange. Here is the end of the world, and what the ant cares is how to get with this little piece of-- of-- I don't know, whatever she had there-- to her place. And somehow, I remember this so well, looking at this ant. It bring me some-- some, you know, feeling of security that after all, the nature has its own ways. Maybe indifferent to our problems, but somehow there is an order in nature. And then I wasn't so frightened anymore. Well, but anyway, by the next air raid, we get lost with my mother-- and my mother was somewhere else. And I was with my father, and I was, as matter of fact, in my mother long coat, because it was cold and-- so anyway, we went near Lemberg. And then, as soon we arrived there-- and there were masses of refugees from all over. And then the Russian came.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we did not even know what is going on. Why the Russian came? Why they came from the east, and went up to the Vistula River, also? So anyway, there we were. And we went to a very historic old town, small town, [NON-ENGLISH] near Lemberg.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there was, as a matter of fact, in this part of Poland, there was enormous mass of Jewish community-- the Orthodox or Hasidic Jewish community, where they were all in these long things, and they were the famous rabbis in Belz. And-- and the rabbi said that Jews should not worry, and should not do anything, because everything will be all right.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the Jews believed very much that-- that rabbis. And I couldn't believe that the whole world is going so upside down, any moment can the German come here-- if not Germans, the Russian-- and he says that everything is all right? He really said that everything is all right, but somehow he very soon found himself in America, and the poor Jews were there.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's not that he wanted to, you know, be a traitor to them. No. It just happened.

speaker: interviewer
text: So anyway, I went-- my father who was so about the education, he said, war or not war, you have to go to school. Here is gymnasium open-- go to school. So I went to school. And in this school, well, the professor from Latin, he said, I was a model to other students. Look how they teach the students Latin in Krakow. Beautiful! Because I knew how to translate, and I knew by heart Cicero and this and that. And I was talking this 'Quo usque tandem abutere, Catilina, patientia nostra'-- you know, well, that famous oration of Caesar.

speaker: interviewee
text: But then the math teacher said, they never taught them maths in Krakow. Never! She does not know how to do the logarithm. And I really didn't. Well, but anyway, very soon we found out that my mother returned back to Krakow, and we decide to go back. So we came back and then there were Russian here, and we had to cross the bridge between the Russian and the German. And the Russians said, oh, you stupid people. There was plenty of refugees who wanted to go back. You go to the German. I said, we are going home. We are not going to the Germans. As soon we crossed this bridge, the German took all of us into a fort, and locked us up there, and we did not know why and what for. Well, my father--

speaker: interviewer
text: When was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was right away in maybe November 19-- 1939. And then my father spoke perfect German, so he was able to-- to talk with them and find out and took care of all of us-- we were about 100 or 200. I spoke German, too, very well, because one of my nannies first when I was a little girl was German. But then eventually, they took us into the cattle cars and so we arrived in Krakow after two weeks.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that was about what month of '39?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was end of November, '39. And then I notice how that Krakow looked like, with its swastikas, flags, all over the royal castle and this and that. So, and of course, no school for the Jews. No school.

speaker: interviewer
text: So since-- except Hebrew gymnasium-- so I went to Hebrew gymnasium, but maybe for two weeks. And then it was closed. And then, so we begin to attend clandestine classes-- one's in this house, one's in another house. You know, people believe-- our parents always believe in education, and no matter what.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, so, this is what we did. But then in December, at the beginning of December, we had to have this armbands with David star. Blue David stars.

speaker: interviewer
text: So early December 1939?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1939. And, well, this was when some friends in this clandestine school, they said, oh, I don't look like a Jew-- Jewish girl, I'm not going to have this armband. Well, I also did not look like a Jewish girl, but I said if everybody has to have, maybe I should, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I came back home and, I said to my parents, what about this armband? Maybe I am not going to wear it. And suddenly my father said, if everybody's wearing this Jewish armband, I'm going to wear it, too. I don't want to have a different fate than anybody else. How did it feel to be persecuted merely because of your religion?

speaker: interviewee
text: I even did not consider this as a religion, because they did not talk about religion. The German talk about the race-- you know, race. Said we are of different-- different people-- physically different people. Well, and therefore they said, but we just simply couldn't understand-- how come? Well, we are the same. No, you are not the same. And then very soon I-- we found out that-- that the streetcars were divided for Jews and for not Jews. As a matter of fact, they were not seats for Jews. They were for Aryan-- Aryan, people of Aryan race, and for the Jews. You know, to me, it was so strange that when I came in 1960, 1959, 1960 to Texas, and they were still in Fort Worth, there were the buses were divided. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I-- when I noticed this, I was furious. And I went straight to that divided, in showing of solidarity, there where the black people. And there I sit. And at the next stop, a big black woman came and she starts yelling at me and chased me out of there! And I couldn't understand why she's so angry with me. Here I want to show my solidarity with her. But she was right-- I was taking her place, because I was sitting there and she had none. And therefore-- but then I thought about this tram, streetcars in Krakow, which were divided. They were-- and then one after another, one after another. Then there was that everybody should work. Well, we already felt very bad. First of all, my brother was, at this time, we already knew that he's in POW camp. But the bank disappeared-- National Polish Bank, and savings of my parents with this, and his job, too. And therefore, they were-- and the tenants in our house, they were all poor, they did not have money, so actually this becomes the way down. And we already began then to sell whatever we had left. And then my parents were working on some very, well, little jobs, and then they decide that I should learn something and I wanted to make some money, so I went to work in a pharmacy, by a pharmacist who was my parents' friend.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your-- did your father attempt, with all these-- these-- these difficulties, attempt to arrange for any false papers?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Many of our Polish friends wanted to take us all to arrange for this. But somehow, my parents were against it. Number one, they knew that it was in Poland when you helped Jews, or hid Jews, and the German found out, these people were also killed. It was a death sentence on those people who helped Jews, as well as for the Jews. Number two, in order to be easier, we had to be not together. And somehow, I don't know, it was maybe to us not good, but it was unconceivable that we should part and be different. And then even to endanger other people, not to mention that our-- at this time, well, financial situation was not that good. So parents didn't want it. Besides, as many people, my parents thought that this war could not be so different than the First World War. During First World War, front was front and armies were armies. But in-- there was nothing war against woman, against children, against older people, and against civilian. And during First World War, the universities were open and everything was still going on. But this war was different, very different.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was it that you were first required to wear the armbands?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, at the end of November and the end of December.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the first time that you-- that you saw the Germans enter the town was, just by way of backtracking for a moment, was when-- the very first glimpse of them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very first glimpse of them, it was when we were crossing-- first of all, we saw the German--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, when they entered, actually?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, but-- well, when we were crossing the rivers and coming from east Poland to-- to Krakow.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I noticed them.

speaker: interviewer
text: And-- and when-- when was the ghetto set up?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ghetto was set up in 1941.

speaker: interviewer
text: About what month?

speaker: interviewee
text: March.

speaker: interviewer
text: March, 1941.

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember this exact.

speaker: interviewer
text: When were you notified that you had to move?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was about two months or three months earlier that the Jews should go to-- to-- it will be ghetto. No, but it was, first we were getting Kennkarted, the IDs. We had to register, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was that called? What were the IDs called?

speaker: interviewee
text: Kennkarte. Kenn, it means-- well, like ID. Karte, it was large like this, with photograph. I have photograph from-- from that Kennkarte. We had to register. And while this registration, for some strange reason-- and nobody knew why and why not-- some people got permission to stay in Krakow and some absolutely not. So all the people who did not get this Kennkarte can move outside of Krakow and settle in some suburbs there. And at this time, for about a year, we had the nice free life. However, we were very different. We, who were in Krakow, were told very soon that there will be a ghetto in Krakow, but that ghetto where the Germans did, it was not in this part of Krakow, which was traditionally, well, Jewish, where Jews lived there. We did not live in this, we were in a different part. But they decide to make in the poorest section of Krakow, and therefore, well, when it came the day when we were supposed to move there it looked really strange. On one side of the street you saw masses of people coming from this part to Krakow. On the other side of the street, masses of people coming from Krakow to this part where was supposed to be ghetto. And there were just a few streets, not too much. It was a very small place. And in the middle of the streets, it was all one.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the dates you were notified you had to go was when, again? The--

speaker: interviewee
text: The day we had to be in the ghetto was about, I think, 18 or, or so of March, 1941.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how much time were you given? How much notification did you have?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, this was-- at this time it was quite a long notification, I think about two weeks or so, because they had to take care of this people who were not Jewish in this part of Krakow where the ghetto was, to relocate them. And so therefore, it was not just two days or three days, no. It was--

speaker: interviewer
text: How much did you take with you? How much were you able to take with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we had, like, platform wagon, and well, from four or five rooms you could take just the most important things.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever return home again?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, but that part of our things what we had, we couldn't take with us, we give to our nanny. And, for example, this carpet she had. The things which are in the china cabinet, some of them-- they were, she took them and kept them for us.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long after you arrived at the ghetto did the walls go up?

speaker: interviewee
text: They started already. They were finished in about two or three months.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that would be what date?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, 1941.

speaker: interviewer
text: May of which-- what month?

speaker: interviewee
text: Somehow-- summer.

speaker: interviewer
text: Summer. Thank you, we're going to switch rolls now.
